complaint.Proposition: By May 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, Russia and Ukraine had officially reached a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict, not merely an informal agreement, humanitarian pause, limited sector-specific ceasefire, or future framework.

payload.text:
Council members, this case turns on a narrow but important distinction: whether Russia and Ukraine had, by the May 31 deadline, officially reached and publicly announced a mutually agreed halt in military engagement that was general enough to qualify, or whether the May arrangement was merely informal, humanitarian, sector-specific, or only a future framework.

The plaintiff's theory is straightforward. The record already points to an official, public, mutually accepted May 9-May 11 ceasefire arrangement. It was not a Black Sea-only, energy-infrastructure-only, or private diplomatic aspiration. The preserved market rule asks for a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement, and it expressly allows resolution by either official announcements from both sides or a wide consensus of credible reporting that an official ceasefire agreement had been reached. The record contains both the beginning of an official-source chain and a wide credible-reporting chain.

The official-source record is significant. It preserves a Russian presidential-site source through Yury Ushakov stating that, on President Putin's instructions, the Russian side considered acceptable the U.S.-proposed initiative concerning a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine, and that the key agreement was a May 9-May 11 prisoner exchange during the ceasefire period. That is not a rumor or trader commentary; it is an official Russian account of acceptance. On Ukraine, the present packet is less complete because the direct presidential decree URL was inaccessible in the environment, but the packet preserves official-adjacent Ukrinform reporting that a presidential decree existed and that Ukraine had received Russia's agreement to the 1,000-for-1,000 exchange, alongside reporting that President Zelenskyy confirmed the ceasefire arrangement.

The secondary-reporting file matters because the market rule itself permits a wide consensus of credible media reporting as an evidentiary path. The packet summarizes Reuters, Guardian, NPR, CBC, and ISW as reporting that Russia and Ukraine confirmed a U.S.-brokered three-day ceasefire from May 9 to May 11, with suspension of kinetic activity and a 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner exchange. This is not one stray article; the packet presents a consistent multi-source account, while also preserving adverse details for the council to weigh.

Those adverse details define the real defense case. The ceasefire was temporary. It was tied to Victory Day, Red Square/parade concerns, and a prisoner exchange. The Russian official statement frames the initiative as for the purpose of conducting that exchange. The Ukrainian decree material preserved through Ukrinform appears narrower as to Red Square targeting during the parade. The record also identifies missing direct captures of Zelenskyy's Telegram or X post, the Ukrainian presidential decree, Trump's original announcement or White House record, and battlefield monitoring or violation evidence. The plaintiff does not ask the council to ignore those gaps.

But those gaps do not defeat the proposition at the opening stage. A general pause can be temporary. A ceasefire can include a prisoner exchange and still be a halt in military engagement if the agreed pause is not limited to a single corridor, a single weapons class, one sea lane, or one infrastructure category. The key question will be whether the May 9-May 11 arrangement suspended military engagement generally, even if briefly and even if imperfectly observed. The preserved reporting of a suspension of all kinetic activity is therefore central. So is the official Russian acknowledgment of a ceasefire between the two states during the same dated period.

The plaintiff expects the later proof to focus on four issues: first, direct captures or faithful extracts of the Ukrainian and U.S. announcements; second, the full official Russian source already identified in the packet; third, credible reports or monitoring material about whether the announced halt was theater-wide in scope; and fourth, any post-period official statements or violation reports that show whether alleged breaches were treated as violations of a ceasefire rather than evidence no agreement existed.

At this point, the packet supports the plaintiff more than the defense. It shows an official Russian acceptance, an official-adjacent Ukrainian confirmation path, and a broad credible-media consensus that both parties agreed to a dated ceasefire suspending kinetic activity. The defense has real arguments about limited purpose and implementation, but on a preponderance standard the decisive question is whether an official, public, mutually agreed general halt was reached by the deadline. The plaintiff will show that it was.

Council members, the defense opening is simple: this case is not resolved by the word "ceasefire" appearing in headlines. The proposition requires an official, public, mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict. It expressly excludes humanitarian pauses, informal arrangements, and limited agreements. On the present record, plaintiff has not yet carried that burden.

The starting packet gives plaintiff a surface-level story: several media summaries described a May 9-May 11 ceasefire, a prisoner exchange, and confirmations by both sides. But the same packet also identifies the problems that matter under the actual rule. The official Russian source preserved in the record frames the initiative as a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine "for the purpose of conducting a prisoner exchange," with the key agreement being a 1,000-for-1,000 exchange during the May 9-May 11 period. That is material because a prisoner-exchange pause is exactly the kind of limited or humanitarian arrangement the rule tells the council not to count unless it also proves a general halt in military engagement.

The Ukrainian official-source path is weaker for plaintiff, not stronger. The packet identifies an official presidential decree path, but the captured substance through Ukrinform is narrow: it concerns allowing the May 9 Moscow parade and excluding Red Square from Ukrainian weapons targeting during the parade. The packet itself says that decree does not, by itself, state a theater-wide halt in military engagement. That is not a minor provenance issue. It is the missing link between a limited parade/security accommodation and the market's required general pause in the conflict.

The market-page evidence also deserves careful weight. It preserves the rule and reports that the platform outcome was Yes, but the packet warns that platform assertions and after-the-fact context are not independent proof of official mutuality, theater-wide scope, implementation, or media reliability. The arbitration is about the factual condition, not whether Polymarket or traders treated it as satisfied.

So the real dispute is scope and official mutuality. Did both governments publicly agree to stop military engagement generally, across the conflict, even briefly? Or did they agree to a three-day, Victory Day and prisoner-exchange arrangement, with a geographically narrow Ukrainian decree and ongoing accusations or operations around the period? The present record contains enough to investigate, but not enough to collapse those categories.

The defense expects the decisive proof to be direct captures of the Russian and Ukrainian official statements, the original U.S. announcement if plaintiff relies on it, Zelenskyy's original Telegram or X confirmation, and monitoring or official post-period material showing whether hostilities actually paused generally or continued in material ways. If those sources show a clear, mutual, theater-wide commitment, plaintiff may have a case. If they show a prisoner swap, parade security carveout, temporary symbolism, or violated/continued operations without a real general pause, the proposition fails.

At opening, the council should hold plaintiff to the rule's exclusions. A temporary ceasefire can sometimes qualify, but a temporary label is not enough. The record's primary-source chain presently points to limited purpose and incomplete custody, while the broader affirmative case rests mainly on secondary summaries and platform context. On a preponderance standard, the defense will show that plaintiff has not established the required official, publicly announced, mutually agreed general halt in military engagement by the deadline.

technical_reports.summary: Before argument, plaintiff refreshed the record, reviewed both openings, attempted live retrieval of the Kremlin official page identified in the packet, and searched/captured corroborating secondary sources. The Kremlin live curl did not return usable body text in this environment, so plaintiff relies on the already visible official-source packet for Russian official content. Plaintiff submitted three compact source extracts: Al Jazeera/Reuters, Defense News, and Ukrainska Pravda. Remaining gaps are direct Zelenskyy X/Telegram, Trump Truth Social or White House capture, direct Ukrainian decree text, and battlefield monitoring. Those gaps affect implementation and provenance weight but do not outweigh the admitted official-source and credible-reporting consensus on the agreement reached.
technical_reports.title: Plaintiff source-search ledger

payload.text:
Council members, the preponderance now favors the proposition.

The market rule in ev_f841be6ff80d_market-page asks whether, by May 31, there was an official ceasefire agreement: a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement between Russia and Ukraine. It requires a general pause, and excludes informal, humanitarian-only, energy-only, Black Sea-only, or future-framework arrangements. The plaintiff accepts that standard. The evidence meets it because the May 9-11 arrangement was publicly announced, accepted by both sides, and described as a suspension of kinetic activity in the war, not merely a narrow corridor or sector.

Start with official mutuality. The packet's official-source record, ev_959b165782b3_official-source-record, preserves the Russian presidential-site account from Yury Ushakov. Russia did not merely acknowledge rumors; the Russian side said, on President Putin's instructions, it considered acceptable the U.S.-proposed initiative concerning a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine, and identified the May 9-11 ceasefire period and 1,000-for-1,000 exchange. That is official Russian acceptance.

Ukraine-side proof is less direct than ideal, but it is sufficient under the rule's alternative path. The same official-source packet preserves Ukrinform's account that Ukraine had received Russia's agreement and that a Ukrainian presidential decree existed. More importantly, the newly submitted corroborating sources converge. The Al Jazeera/Reuters extract, ev_eed9632fae9a_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-eed9632fae9a, reports that Zelenskyy confirmed a three-day truce, that Ushakov said agreement was reached through U.S. contacts with Kyiv, and that Trump described the ceasefire as including suspension of all kinetic activity and a 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner swap. Defense News, ev_039a756c335a_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-039a756c335a, reports separate statements from Trump and Zelenskyy including a ceasefire through May 11 and the same exchange, with Trump thanking both Putin and Zelenskyy for agreeing to the pause. Ukrainska Pravda, ev_84697b662d42_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-84697b662d42, adds Ukraine-side reporting that Zelenskyy confirmed Trump's May 9-11 ceasefire announcement and that Russia supported the initiative.

That is exactly the kind of wide credible-reporting consensus the market rule permits. The secondary-reporting packet, ev_cf0c77f2fe52_secondary-reporting, already identified Reuters, Guardian, NPR, CBC, and ISW as reporting that both sides confirmed a U.S.-brokered May 9-11 ceasefire, including suspension of kinetic activity. The new exhibits preserve three concrete source extracts behind that consensus.

The defense's strongest point is not ignored: this was temporary, connected to Victory Day, Red Square/parade security, and a prisoner exchange. But those facts do not make it nonqualifying. The rule does not require a peace settlement, indefinite truce, or perfect battlefield compliance. It requires a mutually agreed halt in military engagement that is general in scope. The most probative phrase in the admitted evidence is not simply "prisoner exchange"; it is the reported suspension of all kinetic activity. A prisoner swap may be one purpose or component of a ceasefire, but when the parties publicly agree to stop kinetic activity in the war for a dated period, the arrangement is not reduced to a humanitarian corridor.

Nor do possible violations defeat the proposition. Violations may show poor compliance; they do not prove no official agreement was reached. In fact, violation accusations often presuppose an operative ceasefire. The unresolved-gaps packet, ev_41c18aa39f56_unresolved-record-gaps, properly flags missing battlefield monitoring and direct captures. Those gaps affect weight, but the burden is preponderance, not perfect archival completeness. We have official Russian acceptance, Ukraine-side confirmation through multiple credible sources, and repeated independent reporting of the same dated, public, mutual halt.

The market-page file is not offered as independent proof that the event happened; its value is the governing definition. The primary-evidence file, ev_54e94624d245_primary-evidence, is likewise important because it maps the preserved source chain and cautions against overreading platform assertions. Taking those cautions seriously still leaves the same answer: by May 31, the parties had officially reached and publicly announced a May 9-11 ceasefire that suspended military engagement generally, even if briefly and amid later disputes.

On this record, it is more likely than not that the proposition is true.

Targeted source checks focused on the Ukrainian official decree, ISW battlefield monitoring for May 9-11, and official/source-chain gaps identified in the packet. Defense submitted the direct Ukrainian decree page and ISW May 9 and May 10 extracts as evidence. technical_reports.title: Defense source-search ledger

payload.text:
Council members, the plaintiff has proved that a temporary ceasefire was announced and widely reported. That is not the same as proving the proposition. The rule requires a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict, and it expressly excludes humanitarian pauses and similar limited arrangements. The record is more consistent with a limited Victory Day/prisoner-exchange arrangement that did not produce a general halt.

Start with the official Ukrainian source now in the record. DX-1, ev_cf6a5f80e566, is the direct presidential decree identified in the packet. It is not a general ceasefire order. It concerns holding a parade in Moscow, permits the May 9 parade, and excludes a Red Square coordinate square from Ukrainian weapons targeting plans during the parade. Its source-chain value is high because it is a direct official page, but its substantive value is narrow. It proves a parade/security carveout; it does not prove that Ukraine officially agreed to halt military engagement across the conflict.

That fits the Russian official-source record, PX-2/ev_959b165782b3. The Russian statement preserved there says the initiative concerned a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine for the purpose of conducting a prisoner exchange, and identifies the key agreement as a May 9-May 11 exchange of 1,000 prisoners from each side during the ceasefire period. The plaintiff calls that official acceptance. Even if so, the official framing is exchange-centered and time-limited, exactly where the rule’s exclusions matter.

Plaintiff’s three new sources do not cure that problem. PX-6/ev_eed9632 reports a Trump announcement and a reported “suspension of all kinetic activity,” but it is secondary reporting, not a direct Trump, Zelenskyy, or Ukrainian government record. PX-7/ev_039a756c is even more defense-favorable: it says Ukraine publicly framed the proposal more cautiously as part of negotiations over a large-scale prisoner exchange, that Zelenskyy framed it around POW return, and that the proposed ceasefire coincided with Victory Day. PX-8/ev_84697 provides Ukraine-side background confirmation, but its main factual context is post-period friction: the prisoner exchange was stalled, Ukraine had submitted lists, and Ukraine’s ombudsman warned that even when Russia agrees, Ukraine can never be fully sure Russia will honor it.

Most importantly, a “halt in military engagement” must mean engagement actually stops in a general way. The defense submitted ISW battlefield monitoring for the period. DX-3/ev_56be60eae740 reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater throughout the first day of the May 9-11 ceasefire, Russian use of the period for rotations, reinforcements, redeployments, and logistics, and that the reportedly agreed prisoner exchange had not yet occurred as of May 9. DX-2/ev_672e50c14e62 reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater throughout the second day, mutual violation accusations, claimed assaults, artillery, drone strikes, and NASA FIRMS indications that activity decreased but did not cease. That is not a general halt in military engagement. At most, it is a reduced-tempo, contested, temporary arrangement.

Plaintiff says violations often presuppose an agreement. That may answer whether there was a ceasefire label or political announcement. It does not answer whether the arrangement constituted the market’s required general pause. A ceasefire so limited that the official Ukrainian act is Red Square-only, the Russian official purpose is prisoner exchange, and battlefield monitors report continued operations across the theater does not satisfy the qualifying condition by a preponderance.

The unresolved-gaps packet, PX-5/ev_41c18aa39f56, also matters. Direct captures of Zelenskyy’s Telegram/X, Trump’s original post or White House record, battlefield monitoring, and official post-period implementation statements were identified as missing. Plaintiff chose secondary extracts instead of the direct originals for the most important mutuality claims. Those extracts are relevant, but the defense has now supplied the direct Ukrainian decree and battlefield monitoring that the packet said were missing.

So the council can accept that there was public talk of a three-day ceasefire and still reject the proposition. The proposition was drafted to screen out limited pauses. This record shows Victory Day timing, parade safety, prisoner exchange purpose, cautious Ukrainian framing, delayed implementation, and continued theater activity. On preponderance, plaintiff has not shown an official, mutually agreed general pause in the conflict by May 31.

Council members, the defense argument depends on collapsing three different questions: whether an agreement was officially reached, whether every instrument implementing it contained the entire agreement, and whether the ceasefire was perfectly obeyed. The proposition turns on the first and on the scope of the announced obligation, not on flawless performance.

First, the Ukrainian decree does not do the work defense assigns it. DX-1, ev_cf6a5f80e566_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-cf6a5f80e566, is a direct official page and should be credited for what it is: a May 8 decree permitting the May 9 Moscow parade and excluding Red Square from Ukrainian targeting during the parade. But a specific carveout decree is not necessarily the whole ceasefire arrangement. It is an implementation/accommodation instrument. Its silence about the full May 9-11 truce does not erase the separate Ukraine-side confirmations preserved in PX-6, PX-7, PX-8, and the secondary-reporting packet.

Second, defense's own characterization of plaintiff's sources confirms the key point: there was a public, mutual, dated pause. PX-7 says separate Trump and Zelenskyy statements included a ceasefire through May 11 and a 1,000-for-1,000 exchange. PX-8 says Zelenskyy confirmed Trump's announcement regarding a ceasefire with Russia on May 9-11 and that Russia supported the initiative. PX-6 reports Zelenskyy confirmation, Ushakov's agreement account, and the important scope term: suspension of all kinetic activity. That is not a Red Square-only or Black Sea-only arrangement.

Third, the ISW evidence is serious but goes to compliance, not formation. DX-3, ev_56be60eae740_submitted-evidence-06-defendant-56be60eae740, reports continued limited operations and that the prisoner exchange had not yet happened as of May 9. DX-2, ev_672e50c14e62_submitted-evidence-05-defendant-672e50c14e62, reports continued limited operations, mutual violation accusations, and decreased but not ceased activity. Those facts weaken any claim of successful implementation. They do not prove the parties failed to officially reach and announce the ceasefire. In ordinary use, accusing the other side of violating a ceasefire presupposes a ceasefire obligation to violate.

The rule excludes merely humanitarian or limited sector-specific pauses. It does not exclude every ceasefire that has a humanitarian component, prisoner-exchange component, ceremonial context, or later violations. If the announced obligation is to suspend kinetic activity in the war, the existence of a prisoner swap and Victory Day timing does not reduce it to a humanitarian corridor.

The defense has shown why the case is close: the truce was temporary, contested, and imperfect. It has not overcome the admitted official-source and credible-reporting consensus that, by the deadline, Russia and Ukraine had publicly agreed to a May 9-11 ceasefire described as a suspension of military/kinetic engagement. On preponderance, that remains a qualifying general pause.

offered_evidence.label: DX-S1 Direct Ukrainian decree narrow scope
DX-S2 ISW May 9 continued theater operations
DX-S3 ISW May 10 continued theater operations

technical_reports.summary: Defense checked the current record and evidence list before filing. Plaintiff filed a rebuttal but no new source evidence. The surrebuttal therefore relies on already admitted defense evidence and packet source-chain gaps rather than new outside material.
technical_reports.title: Surrebuttal record check

payload.text:
Council members, plaintiff’s rebuttal is right about one thing and wrong about the consequence. The Ukrainian decree need not be the whole arrangement. But plaintiff still must prove more than the formation of some temporary ceasefire label. The proposition requires a halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict.

That is why the defense evidence matters. DX-1, ev_cf6a5f80e566, is the only direct Ukrainian official source now in the record. It is narrow: a May 9 parade permission and a Red Square targeting carveout. Plaintiff asks the council to treat secondary reports of broader Ukraine-side confirmation as enough to overcome the direct official document’s narrow scope. That is possible only if those reports, together with the rest of the record, show a general halt. They do not.

Plaintiff’s formation/compliance distinction also overstates the point. Ordinary violations might not defeat an otherwise qualifying ceasefire. But DX-3, ev_56be60eae740, and DX-2, ev_672e50c14e62, are not just proof of a stray breach. They report continued limited offensive operations across the theater on the first and second days, Russian rotations and logistics activity, mutual accusations of assaults, artillery and drone strikes, and monitoring that activity decreased but did not cease. That is directly probative of whether the arrangement constituted a general pause. A “halt in military engagement” cannot be established by preponderance when the best monitoring evidence says theater-wide military engagement continued.

The source-chain issue remains important. Plaintiff’s strongest phrase, “suspension of all kinetic activity,” comes through secondary reporting of Trump’s announcement. The direct Russian official source frames the initiative as for a prisoner exchange. The direct Ukrainian official decree is Red Square/parade-specific. The direct Zelenskyy post and Trump original remain absent. Under a rule that excludes humanitarian and limited pauses, that evidentiary pattern is not enough.

So the defense is not arguing that every violation defeats every ceasefire. It is arguing that this record shows a temporary, exchange-centered, Victory Day/parade arrangement with continued theater operations. That may be a ceasefire in loose diplomatic language. It is not, more likely than not, the qualifying general pause required by the proposition.

Council members, the record leaves a close but answerable question. The defense proved the May arrangement was temporary, politically timed, connected to a prisoner exchange, and imperfectly observed. It did not prove the absence of a qualifying official ceasefire agreement.

The proposition asks whether, by May 31, Russia and Ukraine had officially reached a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause. The market rule in ev_f841be6ff80d_market-page permits proof by official announcements from both sides or by a wide consensus of credible reporting that an official ceasefire agreement was reached. It does not require a peace settlement, indefinite duration, or perfect performance.

The affirmative record satisfies that standard. The official-source record, ev_959b165782b3_official-source-record, preserves Russian official acceptance: Yury Ushakov, on President Putin's instructions, said Russia considered acceptable the U.S.-proposed initiative concerning a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine and identified the May 9-May 11 ceasefire period. That is official Russian assent.

On Ukraine and mutuality, plaintiff's submitted evidence fills the packet's gap enough for preponderance. The Al Jazeera/Reuters extract, ev_eed9632fae9a_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-eed9632fae9a, reports Zelenskyy confirmation, Ushakov's account that agreement was reached with U.S. contacts with Kyiv, and the key scope term: suspension of all kinetic activity. Defense News, ev_039a756c335a_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-039a756c335a, reports separate Trump and Zelenskyy statements including a ceasefire running through May 11 and a 1,000-for-1,000 exchange, with Trump thanking both Putin and Zelenskyy for agreeing to the pause. Ukrainska Pravda, ev_84697b662d42_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-84697b662d42, gives Ukraine-side reporting that Zelenskyy confirmed Trump's May 9-11 ceasefire announcement and that Russia supported the initiative. The secondary-reporting packet, ev_cf0c77f2fe52_secondary-reporting, shows this was not isolated: Reuters, Guardian, NPR, CBC, and ISW were all identified as reporting both-side confirmation.

The defense's best evidence changes weight, not result. The Ukrainian decree, ev_cf6a5f80e566_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-cf6a5f80e566, is direct and narrow: a Red Square/parade targeting carveout. But even the defense now acknowledges it need not be the whole arrangement. A specific decree implementing parade safety does not negate separate public confirmation of a three-day ceasefire.

The ISW exhibits, ev_56be60eae740_submitted-evidence-06-defendant-56be60eae740 and ev_672e50c14e62_submitted-evidence-05-defendant-672e50c14e62, are the strongest adverse proof. They show continued limited operations, mutual violation accusations, rotations and logistics, and activity that decreased but did not cease. That makes the ceasefire imperfect and contested. It does not make it nonexistent. Violation accusations naturally presuppose a ceasefire obligation; they do not erase the public agreement that created it.

The rule excludes humanitarian-only and sector-specific pauses. But this record contains an announced suspension of kinetic activity in the war, not merely a single corridor, sea lane, energy facility, or future framework. The prisoner swap and Victory Day context explain why the parties agreed; they do not shrink the announced halt into a nonqualifying humanitarian corridor.

Preponderance means more likely than not. The official Russian source plus the credible, multi-source reporting of Zelenskyy confirmation and suspension of kinetic activity makes it more likely than not that, by the deadline, Russia and Ukraine had officially reached and publicly announced a mutually agreed general pause in military engagement. The council should find the proposition true.

Council members, the plaintiff’s closing fairly frames why this case is close. There was public reporting of a temporary May 9-May 11 ceasefire, and the defense does not need to deny that the word “ceasefire” was used. The proposition, however, is narrower and harder: plaintiff must prove an official, public, mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict, not a humanitarian, ceremonial, exchange-centered, or otherwise limited arrangement.

The admitted record does not meet that burden.

The governing rule in ev_f841be6ff80d_market-page requires a general pause and excludes humanitarian pauses and limited agreements. That language is decisive. A prisoner swap can coexist with a qualifying ceasefire, but the record must still show a general halt. Here, the direct official sources point the other way. The Russian official-source packet, ev_959b165782b3_official-source-record, frames the initiative as concerning a ceasefire for the purpose of conducting a prisoner exchange and identifies the key agreement as the 1,000-for-1,000 exchange during May 9-May 11. The direct Ukrainian official source, ev_cf6a5f80e566_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-cf6a5f80e566, is narrower still: it permits the Moscow parade and excludes Red Square coordinates from Ukrainian targeting plans during the parade. It does not announce a theater-wide halt.

Plaintiff says the decree may be only one implementation instrument. Perhaps. But plaintiff bears the burden, and the direct Zelenskyy post and direct Trump original remain missing. What plaintiff offers instead are secondary extracts. Those extracts are relevant, but they carry the limits they state. Defense News, ev_039a756c335a, reports that Ukraine framed the proposed ceasefire cautiously as part of prisoner-exchange negotiations, that Zelenskyy framed the agreement around POW return, and that the proposal coincided with Victory Day. Ukrainska Pravda, ev_84697b662d42, confirms Ukraine-side reporting but also records post-period friction over the prisoner exchange and Ukraine’s skepticism that Russia would honor agreements.

The battlefield evidence is the strongest reason the proposition fails. ISW’s May 9 assessment, ev_56be60eae740, reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater on the first day, Russian rotations, reinforcements, redeployments and logistics, and no completed prisoner exchange as of May 9. ISW’s May 10 assessment, ev_672e50c14e62, reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater on the second day, mutual accusations, artillery and drone activity, and monitoring showing activity decreased but did not cease. That is not a general halt in military engagement. It is the opposite: a reduced and disputed tempo during a temporary political arrangement.

Plaintiff’s best answer is that violations presuppose a ceasefire obligation. That may establish a label. It does not establish the qualifying condition. The proposition does not ask whether there was any announcement capable of being violated; it asks whether the parties reached a halt that constituted a general pause in the conflict. The record’s own tension file, ev_41c18aa39f56, recognizes the defense case: Victory Day, Red Square, prisoner-exchange, and alleged-violation context may prevent the arrangement from satisfying the general-pause and non-humanitarian exclusions.

On preponderance, the more grounded reading is this: Russia and Ukraine publicly entertained a short, U.S.-brokered, prisoner-exchange and parade-related ceasefire arrangement; credible media described it broadly; but the direct official Ukrainian instrument was geographically and temporally narrow, the Russian official framing was exchange-centered, and military engagement continued across the theater. Plaintiff has shown a temporary ceasefire label. It has not shown the required general pause. The council should find the proposition not proven.

The record shows official Russian acceptance, multiple credible media reports confirming a mutual agreement to suspend kinetic activity, and a clear public announcement by both sides. The arrangement, while temporary and limited in scope, constituted a general pause in military engagement as required by the definition.

The record shows a temporary, prisoner-exchange-focused arrangement with continued theater-wide military operations. arguments.text:
Council members, the preponderance now favors the proposition.

The market rule in ev_f841be6ff80d_market-page asks whether, by May 31, there was an official ceasefire agreement: a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement between Russia and Ukraine. It requires a general pause, and excludes informal, humanitarian-only, energy-only, Black Sea-only, or future-framework arrangements. The plaintiff accepts that standard. The evidence meets it because the May 9-11 arrangement was publicly announced, accepted by both sides, and described as a suspension of kinetic activity in the war, not merely a narrow corridor or sector.

Start with official mutuality. The packet's official-source record, ev_959b165782b3_official-source-record, preserves the Russian presidential-site account from Yury Ushakov. Russia did not merely acknowledge rumors; the Russian side said, on President Putin's instructions, it considered acceptable the U.S.-proposed initiative concerning a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine, and identified the May 9-11 ceasefire period and 1,000-for-1,000 exchange. That is official Russian acceptance.

Ukraine-side proof is less direct than ideal, but it is sufficient under the rule's alternative path. The same official-source packet preserves Ukrinform's account that Ukraine had received Russia's agreement and that a Ukrainian presidential decree existed. More importantly, the newly submitted corroborating sources converge. The Al Jazeera/Reuters extract, ev_eed9632fae9a_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-eed9632fae9a, reports that Zelenskyy confirmed a three-day truce, that Ushakov said agreement was reached through U.S. contacts with Kyiv, and that Trump described the ceasefire as including suspension of all kinetic activity and a 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner swap. Defense News, ev_039a756c335a_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-039a756c335a, reports separate statements from Trump and Zelenskyy including a ceasefire through May 11 and the same exchange, with Trump thanking both Putin and Zelenskyy for agreeing to the pause. Ukrainska Pravda, ev_84697b662d42_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-84697b662d42, adds Ukraine-side reporting that Zelenskyy confirmed Trump's May 9-11 ceasefire announcement and that Russia supported the initiative.

That is exactly the kind of wide credible-reporting consensus the market rule permits. The secondary-reporting packet, ev_cf0c77f2fe52_secondary-reporting, already identified Reuters, Guardian, NPR, CBC, and ISW as reporting that both sides confirmed a U.S.-brokered May 9-11 ceasefire, including suspension of kinetic activity. The new exhibits preserve three concrete source extracts behind that consensus.

The defense's strongest point is not ignored: this was temporary, connected to Victory Day, Red Square/parade security, and a prisoner exchange. But those facts do not make it nonqualifying. The rule does not require a peace settlement, indefinite truce, or perfect battlefield compliance. It requires a mutually agreed halt in military engagement that is general in scope. The most probative phrase in the admitted evidence is not simply "prisoner exchange"; it is the reported suspension of all kinetic activity. A prisoner swap may be one purpose or component of a ceasefire, but when the parties publicly agree to stop kinetic activity in the war for a dated period, the arrangement is not reduced to a humanitarian corridor.

Nor do possible violations defeat the proposition. Violations may show poor compliance; they do not prove no official agreement was reached. In fact, violation accusations often presuppose an operative ceasefire. The unresolved-gaps packet, ev_41c18aa39f56_unresolved-record-gaps, properly flags missing battlefield monitoring and direct captures. Those gaps affect weight, but the burden is preponderance, not perfect archival completeness. We have official Russian acceptance, Ukraine-side confirmation through multiple credible sources, and repeated independent reporting of the same dated, public, mutual halt.

The market-page file is not offered as independent proof that the event happened; its value is the governing definition. The primary-evidence file, ev_54e94624d245_primary-evidence, is likewise important because it maps the preserved source chain and cautions against overreading platform assertions. Taking those cautions seriously still leaves the same answer: by May 31, the parties had officially reached and publicly announced a May 9-11 ceasefire that suspended military engagement generally, even if briefly and amid later disputes.

On this record, it is more likely than not that the proposition is true.

Council members, the plaintiff has proved that a temporary ceasefire was announced and widely reported. That is not the same as proving the proposition. The rule requires a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict, and it expressly excludes humanitarian pauses and similar limited arrangements. The record is more consistent with a limited Victory Day/prisoner-exchange arrangement that did not produce a general halt.

Start with the official Ukrainian source now in the record. DX-1, ev_cf6a5f80e566, is the direct presidential decree identified in the packet. It is not a general ceasefire order. It concerns holding a parade in Moscow, permits the May 9 parade, and excludes a Red Square coordinate square from Ukrainian weapons targeting plans during the parade. Its source-chain value is high because it is a direct official page, but its substantive value is narrow. It proves a parade/security carveout; it does not prove that Ukraine officially agreed to halt military engagement across the conflict.

That fits the Russian official-source record, PX-2/ev_959b165782b3. The Russian statement preserved there says the initiative concerned a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine for the purpose of conducting a prisoner exchange, and identifies the key agreement as a May 9-May 11 exchange of 1,000 prisoners from each side during the ceasefire period. The plaintiff calls that official acceptance. Even if so, the official framing is exchange-centered and time-limited, exactly where the rule’s exclusions matter.

Plaintiff’s three new sources do not cure that problem. PX-6/ev_eed9632 reports a Trump announcement and a reported “suspension of all kinetic activity,” but it is secondary reporting, not a direct Trump, Zelenskyy, or Ukrainian government record. PX-7/ev_039a756c is even more defense-favorable: it says Ukraine publicly framed the proposal more cautiously as part of negotiations over a large-scale prisoner exchange, that Zelenskyy framed it around POW return, and that the proposed ceasefire coincided with Victory Day. PX-8/ev_84697 provides Ukraine-side background confirmation, but its main factual context is post-period friction: the prisoner exchange was stalled, Ukraine had submitted lists, and Ukraine’s ombudsman warned that even when Russia agrees, Ukraine can never be fully sure Russia will honor it.

Most importantly, a “halt in military engagement” must mean engagement actually stops in a general way. The defense submitted ISW battlefield monitoring for the period. DX-3/ev_56be60eae740 reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater throughout the first day of the May 9-11 ceasefire, Russian use of the period for rotations, reinforcements, redeployments, and logistics, and that the reportedly agreed prisoner exchange had not yet occurred as of May 9. DX-2/ev_672e50c14e62 reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater throughout the second day, mutual violation accusations, claimed assaults, artillery, drone strikes, and NASA FIRMS indications that activity decreased but did not cease. That is not a general halt in military engagement. At most, it is a reduced-tempo, contested, temporary arrangement.

Plaintiff says violations often presuppose an agreement. That may answer whether there was a ceasefire label or political announcement. It does not answer whether the arrangement constituted the market’s required general pause. A ceasefire so limited that the official Ukrainian act is Red Square-only, the Russian official purpose is prisoner exchange, and battlefield monitors report continued operations across the theater does not satisfy the qualifying condition by a preponderance.

The unresolved-gaps packet, PX-5/ev_41c18aa39f56, also matters. Direct captures of Zelenskyy’s Telegram/X, Trump’s original post or White House record, battlefield monitoring, and official post-period implementation statements were identified as missing. Plaintiff chose secondary extracts instead of the direct originals for the most important mutuality claims. Those extracts are relevant, but the defense has now supplied the direct Ukrainian decree and battlefield monitoring that the packet said were missing.

So the council can accept that there was public talk of a three-day ceasefire and still reject the proposition. The proposition was drafted to screen out limited pauses. This record shows Victory Day timing, parade safety, prisoner exchange purpose, cautious Ukrainian framing, delayed implementation, and continued theater activity. On preponderance, plaintiff has not shown an official, mutually agreed general pause in the conflict by May 31.

case.caption: Claimant v. Respondent

openings.text:
Council members, this case turns on a narrow but important distinction: whether Russia and Ukraine had, by the May 31 deadline, officially reached and publicly announced a mutually agreed halt in military engagement that was general enough to qualify, or whether the May arrangement was merely informal, humanitarian, sector-specific, or only a future framework.

The plaintiff's theory is straightforward. The record already points to an official, public, mutually accepted May 9-May 11 ceasefire arrangement. It was not a Black Sea-only, energy-infrastructure-only, or private diplomatic aspiration. The preserved market rule asks for a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement, and it expressly allows resolution by either official announcements from both sides or a wide consensus of credible reporting that an official ceasefire agreement had been reached. The record contains both the beginning of an official-source chain and a wide credible-reporting chain.

The official-source record is significant. It preserves a Russian presidential-site source through Yury Ushakov stating that, on President Putin's instructions, the Russian side considered acceptable the U.S.-proposed initiative concerning a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine, and that the key agreement was a May 9-May 11 prisoner exchange during the ceasefire period. That is not a rumor or trader commentary; it is an official Russian account of acceptance. On Ukraine, the present packet is less complete because the direct presidential decree URL was inaccessible in the environment, but the packet preserves official-adjacent Ukrinform reporting that a presidential decree existed and that Ukraine had received Russia's agreement to the 1,000-for-1,000 exchange, alongside reporting that President Zelenskyy confirmed the ceasefire arrangement.

The secondary-reporting file matters because the market rule itself permits a wide consensus of credible media reporting as an evidentiary path. The packet summarizes Reuters, Guardian, NPR, CBC, and ISW as reporting that Russia and Ukraine confirmed a U.S.-brokered three-day ceasefire from May 9 to May 11, with suspension of kinetic activity and a 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner exchange. This is not one stray article; the packet presents a consistent multi-source account, while also preserving adverse details for the council to weigh.

Those adverse details define the real defense case. The ceasefire was temporary. It was tied to Victory Day, Red Square/parade concerns, and a prisoner exchange. The Russian official statement frames the initiative as for the purpose of conducting that exchange. The Ukrainian decree material preserved through Ukrinform appears narrower as to Red Square targeting during the parade. The record also identifies missing direct captures of Zelenskyy's Telegram or X post, the Ukrainian presidential decree, Trump's original announcement or White House record, and battlefield monitoring or violation evidence. The plaintiff does not ask the council to ignore those gaps.

But those gaps do not defeat the proposition at the opening stage. A general pause can be temporary. A ceasefire can include a prisoner exchange and still be a halt in military engagement if the agreed pause is not limited to a single corridor, a single weapons class, one sea lane, or one infrastructure category. The key question will be whether the May 9-May 11 arrangement suspended military engagement generally, even if briefly and even if imperfectly observed. The preserved reporting of a suspension of all kinetic activity is therefore central. So is the official Russian acknowledgment of a ceasefire between the two states during the same dated period.

The plaintiff expects the later proof to focus on four issues: first, direct captures or faithful extracts of the Ukrainian and U.S. announcements; second, the full official Russian source already identified in the packet; third, credible reports or monitoring material about whether the announced halt was theater-wide in scope; and fourth, any post-period official statements or violation reports that show whether alleged breaches were treated as violations of a ceasefire rather than evidence no agreement existed.

At this point, the packet supports the plaintiff more than the defense. It shows an official Russian acceptance, an official-adjacent Ukrainian confirmation path, and a broad credible-media consensus that both parties agreed to a dated ceasefire suspending kinetic activity. The defense has real arguments about limited purpose and implementation, but on a preponderance standard the decisive question is whether an official, public, mutually agreed general halt was reached by the deadline. The plaintiff will show that it was.

Council members, the defense opening is simple: this case is not resolved by the word "ceasefire" appearing in headlines. The proposition requires an official, public, mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict. It expressly excludes humanitarian pauses, informal arrangements, and limited agreements. On the present record, plaintiff has not yet carried that burden.

The starting packet gives plaintiff a surface-level story: several media summaries described a May 9-May 11 ceasefire, a prisoner exchange, and confirmations by both sides. But the same packet also identifies the problems that matter under the actual rule. The official Russian source preserved in the record frames the initiative as a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine "for the purpose of conducting a prisoner exchange," with the key agreement being a 1,000-for-1,000 exchange during the May 9-May 11 period. That is material because a prisoner-exchange pause is exactly the kind of limited or humanitarian arrangement the rule tells the council not to count unless it also proves a general halt in military engagement.

The Ukrainian official-source path is weaker for plaintiff, not stronger. The packet identifies an official presidential decree path, but the captured substance through Ukrinform is narrow: it concerns allowing the May 9 Moscow parade and excluding Red Square from Ukrainian weapons targeting during the parade. The packet itself says that decree does not, by itself, state a theater-wide halt in military engagement. That is not a minor provenance issue. It is the missing link between a limited parade/security accommodation and the market's required general pause in the conflict.

The market-page evidence also deserves careful weight. It preserves the rule and reports that the platform outcome was Yes, but the packet warns that platform assertions and after-the-fact context are not independent proof of official mutuality, theater-wide scope, implementation, or media reliability. The arbitration is about the factual condition, not whether Polymarket or traders treated it as satisfied.

So the real dispute is scope and official mutuality. Did both governments publicly agree to stop military engagement generally, across the conflict, even briefly? Or did they agree to a three-day, Victory Day and prisoner-exchange arrangement, with a geographically narrow Ukrainian decree and ongoing accusations or operations around the period? The present record contains enough to investigate, but not enough to collapse those categories.

The defense expects the decisive proof to be direct captures of the Russian and Ukrainian official statements, the original U.S. announcement if plaintiff relies on it, Zelenskyy's original Telegram or X confirmation, and monitoring or official post-period material showing whether hostilities actually paused generally or continued in material ways. If those sources show a clear, mutual, theater-wide commitment, plaintiff may have a case. If they show a prisoner swap, parade security carveout, temporary symbolism, or violated/continued operations without a real general pause, the proposition fails.

At opening, the council should hold plaintiff to the rule's exclusions. A temporary ceasefire can sometimes qualify, but a temporary label is not enough. The record's primary-source chain presently points to limited purpose and incomplete custody, while the broader affirmative case rests mainly on secondary summaries and platform context. On a preponderance standard, the defense will show that plaintiff has not established the required official, publicly announced, mutually agreed general halt in military engagement by the deadline.

case.proposition: By May 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, Russia and Ukraine had officially reached a publicly announced and mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict, not merely an informal agreement, humanitarian pause, limited sector-specific ceasefire, or future framework.

rebuttals.text:
Council members, the defense argument depends on collapsing three different questions: whether an agreement was officially reached, whether every instrument implementing it contained the entire agreement, and whether the ceasefire was perfectly obeyed. The proposition turns on the first and on the scope of the announced obligation, not on flawless performance.

First, the Ukrainian decree does not do the work defense assigns it. DX-1, ev_cf6a5f80e566_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-cf6a5f80e566, is a direct official page and should be credited for what it is: a May 8 decree permitting the May 9 Moscow parade and excluding Red Square from Ukrainian targeting during the parade. But a specific carveout decree is not necessarily the whole ceasefire arrangement. It is an implementation/accommodation instrument. Its silence about the full May 9-11 truce does not erase the separate Ukraine-side confirmations preserved in PX-6, PX-7, PX-8, and the secondary-reporting packet.

Second, defense's own characterization of plaintiff's sources confirms the key point: there was a public, mutual, dated pause. PX-7 says separate Trump and Zelenskyy statements included a ceasefire through May 11 and a 1,000-for-1,000 exchange. PX-8 says Zelenskyy confirmed Trump's announcement regarding a ceasefire with Russia on May 9-11 and that Russia supported the initiative. PX-6 reports Zelenskyy confirmation, Ushakov's agreement account, and the important scope term: suspension of all kinetic activity. That is not a Red Square-only or Black Sea-only arrangement.

Third, the ISW evidence is serious but goes to compliance, not formation. DX-3, ev_56be60eae740_submitted-evidence-06-defendant-56be60eae740, reports continued limited operations and that the prisoner exchange had not yet happened as of May 9. DX-2, ev_672e50c14e62_submitted-evidence-05-defendant-672e50c14e62, reports continued limited operations, mutual violation accusations, and decreased but not ceased activity. Those facts weaken any claim of successful implementation. They do not prove the parties failed to officially reach and announce the ceasefire. In ordinary use, accusing the other side of violating a ceasefire presupposes a ceasefire obligation to violate.

The rule excludes merely humanitarian or limited sector-specific pauses. It does not exclude every ceasefire that has a humanitarian component, prisoner-exchange component, ceremonial context, or later violations. If the announced obligation is to suspend kinetic activity in the war, the existence of a prisoner swap and Victory Day timing does not reduce it to a humanitarian corridor.

The defense has shown why the case is close: the truce was temporary, contested, and imperfect. It has not overcome the admitted official-source and credible-reporting consensus that, by the deadline, Russia and Ukraine had publicly agreed to a May 9-11 ceasefire described as a suspension of military/kinetic engagement. On preponderance, that remains a qualifying general pause.

submitted_evidence.title: Al Jazeera/Reuters article extract - Trump announces three-day ceasefire in Russia-Ukraine war

submitted_evidence.relevance: Corroborates separate statements by Trump and Zelenskyy, three-day halt beginning May 9, exchange of 1,000 prisoners from each country, and Ukraine's guarded compliance position.

submitted_evidence.relevance: Direct official Ukrainian source identified in the packet. It shows the decree was limited to permitting the May 9 Moscow parade and excluding Red Square coordinates from Ukrainian weapons plans during the parade, rather than announcing a theater-wide halt in military engagement.

submitted_evidence.relevance: Battlefield monitoring/adverse source for the general-pause element. It reports continued limited offensive operations and mutual violation accusations during the alleged ceasefire, supporting the defense argument that the arrangement did not constitute a general halt in military engagement.

submitted_evidence.title: ISW May 10, 2026 assessment - operations continued during May 9-11 ceasefire

submitted_evidence.relevance: Battlefield monitoring for the first day of the alleged May 9-11 ceasefire. It reports continued limited offensive operations and the prisoner exchange had not yet occurred, supporting the defense argument that the arrangement was not a realized general halt.

surrebuttals.text:
Council members, plaintiff’s rebuttal is right about one thing and wrong about the consequence. The Ukrainian decree need not be the whole arrangement. But plaintiff still must prove more than the formation of some temporary ceasefire label. The proposition requires a halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict.

That is why the defense evidence matters. DX-1, ev_cf6a5f80e566, is the only direct Ukrainian official source now in the record. It is narrow: a May 9 parade permission and a Red Square targeting carveout. Plaintiff asks the council to treat secondary reports of broader Ukraine-side confirmation as enough to overcome the direct official document’s narrow scope. That is possible only if those reports, together with the rest of the record, show a general halt. They do not.

Plaintiff’s formation/compliance distinction also overstates the point. Ordinary violations might not defeat an otherwise qualifying ceasefire. But DX-3, ev_56be60eae740, and DX-2, ev_672e50c14e62, are not just proof of a stray breach. They report continued limited offensive operations across the theater on the first and second days, Russian rotations and logistics activity, mutual accusations of assaults, artillery and drone strikes, and monitoring that activity decreased but did not cease. That is directly probative of whether the arrangement constituted a general pause. A “halt in military engagement” cannot be established by preponderance when the best monitoring evidence says theater-wide military engagement continued.

The source-chain issue remains important. Plaintiff’s strongest phrase, “suspension of all kinetic activity,” comes through secondary reporting of Trump’s announcement. The direct Russian official source frames the initiative as for a prisoner exchange. The direct Ukrainian official decree is Red Square/parade-specific. The direct Zelenskyy post and Trump original remain absent. Under a rule that excludes humanitarian and limited pauses, that evidentiary pattern is not enough.

So the defense is not arguing that every violation defeats every ceasefire. It is arguing that this record shows a temporary, exchange-centered, Victory Day/parade arrangement with continued theater operations. That may be a ceasefire in loose diplomatic language. It is not, more likely than not, the qualifying general pause required by the proposition.

technical_reports.summary: Before argument, plaintiff refreshed the record, reviewed both openings, attempted live retrieval of the Kremlin official page identified in the packet, and searched/captured corroborating secondary sources. The Kremlin live curl did not return usable body text in this environment, so plaintiff relies on the already visible official-source packet for Russian official content. Plaintiff submitted three compact source extracts: Al Jazeera/Reuters, Defense News, and Ukrainska Pravda. Remaining gaps are direct Zelenskyy X/Telegram, Trump Truth Social or White House capture, direct Ukrainian decree text, and battlefield monitoring. Those gaps affect implementation and provenance weight but do not outweigh the admitted official-source and credible-reporting consensus on the agreement reached.
technical_reports.title: Plaintiff source-search ledger

technical_reports.summary: Before filing, defense scanned the full current record and read plaintiff's three submitted extracts. Targeted source checks focused on the Ukrainian official decree, ISW battlefield monitoring for May 9-11, and official/source-chain gaps identified in the packet. Defense submitted the direct Ukrainian decree page and ISW May 9 and May 10 extracts as evidence. Remaining gaps are the direct Trump post/White House record, Zelenskyy original social post, and fuller May 11/post-period official statements, but those gaps primarily burden plaintiff's official-mutuality theory.
technical_reports.title: Defense source-search ledger

technical_reports.summary: Defense checked the current record and evidence list before filing. Plaintiff filed a rebuttal but no new source evidence. The surrebuttal therefore relies on already admitted defense evidence and packet source-chain gaps rather than new outside material.
technical_reports.title: Surrebuttal record check
